Apple___Apple_scab.symptoms: Olive-green to brown velvety spots appear on leaves, often followed by yellowing, distortion, premature leaf drop, and scabby fruit lesions.
Apple___Apple_scab.causes: The fungus Venturia inaequalis survives on fallen infected leaves and spreads during cool, wet spring weather.
Apple___Apple_scab.treatment: Remove infected leaves and fallen debris, prune for airflow, and use a locally approved protective fungicide program when scab pressure is high.
Apple___Apple_scab.prevention: Choose resistant varieties where possible, keep the orchard floor clean, avoid dense canopies, and begin monitoring early in the season.

Apple___Black_rot.symptoms: Leaves develop purple-edged brown spots that may look like frog eyes, and fruit can form firm dark rots.
Apple___Black_rot.causes: The fungus Botryosphaeria obtusa survives in cankers, mummified fruit, and dead wood, then spreads by rain splash.
Apple___Black_rot.treatment: Prune out cankers and dead wood, remove mummified fruit, and apply recommended fungicides during susceptible growth stages.
Apple___Black_rot.prevention: Maintain orchard sanitation, prevent tree stress, prune during dry weather, and remove infected material from the site.

Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.symptoms: Bright yellow-orange leaf spots develop, sometimes with raised structures on leaf undersides and reduced fruit quality.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.causes: A rust fungus alternates between apple and juniper or cedar hosts, spreading spores during wet spring conditions.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.treatment: Remove nearby infected juniper galls where practical and use labeled fungicides preventively in high-risk areas.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.prevention: Plant resistant apple cultivars, increase distance from cedar or juniper hosts, and monitor leaves soon after bloom.

Apple___healthy.symptoms: Leaves show normal green color, good shape, and no obvious spotting, rust, blight, or mildew.
Apple___healthy.causes: Healthy leaves usually reflect suitable nutrition, moisture, sunlight, airflow, and low pathogen pressure.
Apple___healthy.treatment: No disease treatment is needed; continue regular monitoring and avoid unnecessary chemical applications.
Apple___healthy.prevention: Keep trees well pruned, remove fallen debris, water consistently, and scout for early pest or disease symptoms.

Blueberry___healthy.symptoms: Leaves appear evenly colored and firm, with no unusual spotting, scorch, curling, or fungal growth.
Blueberry___healthy.causes: Healthy blueberry foliage is supported by acidic soil, steady moisture, balanced nutrition, and good airflow.
Blueberry___healthy.treatment: No disease treatment is required; maintain crop care and monitor for changes.
Blueberry___healthy.prevention: Maintain acidic soil, mulch shallow roots, avoid waterlogging, prune crowded growth, and remove diseased debris promptly.

Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.symptoms: White powdery fungal growth appears on leaves and shoots, with curling, yellowing, and reduced vigor.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.causes: Powdery mildew is favored by susceptible new growth, shade, moderate humidity, and poor air movement.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.treatment: Prune crowded shoots, remove heavily infected growth, and apply sulfur or another labeled fungicide when needed.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.prevention: Improve canopy airflow, avoid excessive nitrogen, plant resistant cultivars where available, and monitor young leaves.

Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.symptoms: Leaves are normally shaped and colored without visible mildew, spotting, scorch, or dieback.
Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.causes: Healthy leaves are associated with good drainage, suitable nutrition, adequate sunlight, and low disease pressure.
Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.treatment: No treatment is needed beyond normal orchard care.
Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.prevention: Prune for airflow, remove fallen leaves and fruit, water during drought, and inspect regularly for early symptoms.

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.disease_name: Corn (maize) - Cercospora leaf spot Gray leaf spot
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.symptoms: Long rectangular gray to tan lesions appear between leaf veins and may merge under heavy disease pressure.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.causes: Cercospora fungi survive in corn residue and spread during warm, humid weather with extended leaf wetness.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.treatment: Use resistant hybrids, rotate away from corn, manage residue, and apply labeled fungicides when disease reaches economic thresholds.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.prevention: Rotate crops, reduce infected residue, select resistant hybrids, and avoid overly dense planting.

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.symptoms: Small cinnamon-brown pustules form on both leaf surfaces and may darken as the disease matures.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.causes: Rust spores blow in from infected areas and develop quickly in cool to moderate, humid conditions.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.treatment: Most cases need no treatment, but fungicides may be justified on susceptible hybrids when disease appears early and pressure is high.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.prevention: Plant resistant hybrids, scout fields regularly, and maintain balanced fertility to reduce crop stress.

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.symptoms: Large cigar-shaped gray-green to tan lesions develop on leaves and can reduce photosynthetic area.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.causes: The fungus Exserohilum turcicum survives in corn residue and spreads in humid, moderate-temperature weather.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.treatment: Use resistant hybrids, rotate crops, manage residue, and apply fungicides when disease risk and crop stage justify it.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.prevention: Rotate away from corn, choose resistant hybrids, bury or break down residue, and scout before tasseling.

Corn_(maize)___healthy.symptoms: Leaves are green and upright with no major lesions, rust pustules, blighting, or nutrient-stress patterns.
Corn_(maize)___healthy.causes: Healthy corn foliage reflects good hybrid selection, balanced fertility, adequate water, and low disease pressure.
Corn_(maize)___healthy.treatment: No disease treatment is required; continue scouting and maintain crop nutrition.
Corn_(maize)___healthy.prevention: Use crop rotation, balanced fertilization, residue management, and regular field monitoring.

Grape___Black_rot.symptoms: Leaves show tan circular spots with dark margins, and berries can shrivel into hard black mummies.
Grape___Black_rot.causes: The fungus Guignardia bidwellii survives in mummified fruit and infected canes, spreading during warm, wet weather.
Grape___Black_rot.treatment: Remove mummified fruit and infected tissue, prune for airflow, and apply preventive fungicides during early growth stages.
Grape___Black_rot.prevention: Sanitize vineyards, train vines for airflow, remove wild grape hosts nearby, and protect young tissue during rainy periods.

Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).symptoms: Leaves may show interveinal striping or scorch, and berries can develop small dark measle-like spots.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).causes: Esca is associated with wood-infecting fungi that colonize pruning wounds and older vine trunks.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).treatment: Remove severely affected vines or cordons, protect pruning wounds where recommended, and avoid spreading infected wood.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).prevention: Prune during dry conditions, disinfect tools when practical, protect large wounds, and maintain vine vigor.

Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.symptoms: Angular brown leaf spots form between veins and may cause blighting, drying, and premature leaf drop.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.causes: The pathogen survives on infected debris and spreads with humid weather and splashing rain.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.treatment: Remove infected leaves, improve canopy airflow, and use locally recommended fungicides when disease pressure increases.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.prevention: Prune and train vines for ventilation, remove debris, avoid overhead irrigation, and scout during wet periods.

Grape___healthy.symptoms: Leaves are normally green and expanded without lesions, mildew, scorch, or abnormal mottling.
Grape___healthy.causes: Healthy grape foliage is supported by good sunlight, airflow, drainage, pruning, and balanced nutrition.
Grape___healthy.treatment: No disease treatment is needed; maintain vineyard hygiene and routine scouting.
Grape___healthy.prevention: Prune for airflow, remove infected debris, manage irrigation, and monitor after rain or high humidity.

Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).symptoms: Leaves show blotchy yellow mottling, shoots may decline, fruit can be small, bitter, misshapen, and unevenly colored.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).causes: Citrus greening is caused by phloem-limited bacteria spread mainly by Asian citrus psyllids and infected plant material.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).treatment: There is no cure; remove infected trees where required, manage psyllids, and use certified disease-free planting stock.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).prevention: Control psyllid vectors, buy certified nursery trees, monitor frequently, and follow regional citrus greening regulations.

Peach___Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Leaves develop small angular dark spots that may fall out, giving a shot-hole appearance, and fruit may develop pits.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.causes: Bacteria spread by wind-driven rain, infected twigs, and wet conditions, especially on susceptible cultivars.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.treatment: Use resistant varieties, prune for airflow, and apply copper or antibiotic products only according to local label guidance.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.prevention: Avoid overhead irrigation, reduce tree stress, select resistant cultivars, and maintain balanced fertility.

Peach___healthy.symptoms: Leaves are evenly colored with no shot holes, spots, curling, mildew, or scorch.
Peach___healthy.causes: Healthy foliage is linked to good site drainage, balanced nutrition, pest monitoring, and suitable pruning.
Peach___healthy.treatment: No disease treatment is needed; continue normal care.
Peach___healthy.prevention: Prune for airflow, irrigate during drought, remove diseased debris, and inspect leaves and fruit regularly.

Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Leaves show small water-soaked spots that turn brown, often with yellow halos; severe infections cause leaf drop.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.causes: Bacteria spread through infected seed or transplants, splashing water, tools, and handling wet plants.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.treatment: Remove infected plants or leaves, avoid overhead watering, improve spacing, and use copper products only as locally recommended.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.prevention: Use certified seed, rotate away from peppers and tomatoes, sanitize tools, and avoid working plants when wet.

Pepper,_bell___healthy.symptoms: Leaves appear firm and green with no water-soaked spots, yellow halos, wilting, or distortion.
Pepper,_bell___healthy.causes: Healthy pepper foliage results from clean planting material, warm conditions, proper irrigation, and balanced nutrients.
Pepper,_bell___healthy.prevention: Rotate crops, avoid overhead watering, mulch to reduce splash, and monitor for insects and early leaf spots.

Potato___Early_blight.symptoms: Older leaves develop brown target-like spots with concentric rings, yellowing, and possible defoliation.
Potato___Early_blight.causes: Alternaria fungi survive in debris and are favored by warm weather, leaf wetness, and plant stress.
Potato___Early_blight.treatment: Remove infected debris, maintain fertility and irrigation, and apply labeled fungicides when disease pressure warrants.
Potato___Early_blight.prevention: Rotate away from potatoes and tomatoes, use healthy seed potatoes, mulch to reduce splash, and avoid plant stress.

Potato___Late_blight.symptoms: Leaves develop water-soaked gray-green lesions that turn brown, often with white growth on undersides in humid weather.
Potato___Late_blight.causes: Phytophthora infestans spreads rapidly in cool, wet conditions through spores from infected plants or tubers.
Potato___Late_blight.treatment: Remove infected plants, avoid moving infected material, and use a recommended fungicide program immediately in outbreak conditions.
Potato___Late_blight.prevention: Plant certified seed, destroy volunteer potatoes, improve airflow, avoid overhead irrigation, and monitor local late blight alerts.

Potato___healthy.symptoms: Leaves are green and vigorous without target spots, water-soaked lesions, wilting, or abnormal yellowing.
Potato___healthy.causes: Healthy potato foliage is supported by clean seed, crop rotation, balanced fertility, and adequate water.
Potato___healthy.prevention: Use certified seed potatoes, rotate crops, hill plants properly, manage irrigation, and scout regularly.

Raspberry___healthy.symptoms: Leaves show normal color and shape without spots, scorch, cane dieback, or abnormal curling.
Raspberry___healthy.causes: Healthy raspberry plants benefit from good drainage, airflow, pruning, and low pest pressure.
Raspberry___healthy.prevention: Prune spent canes, remove infected debris, avoid wet foliage, and maintain weed control and airflow.

Soybean___healthy.symptoms: Leaves are evenly green and well formed with no major spotting, blight, mildew, or insect injury.
Soybean___healthy.causes: Healthy soybean foliage reflects suitable variety choice, balanced fertility, adequate moisture, and low disease pressure.
Soybean___healthy.prevention: Rotate crops, use disease-resistant varieties, monitor fields, manage residue, and avoid unnecessary pesticide use.

Squash___Powdery_mildew.symptoms: White powdery patches appear on leaves and stems, followed by yellowing, browning, and reduced plant vigor.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.causes: Powdery mildew spreads by airborne spores and is favored by dense growth, shade, and moderate humidity.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.treatment: Remove badly infected leaves, improve airflow, and use sulfur, potassium bicarbonate, horticultural oil, or labeled fungicides as appropriate.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.prevention: Plant resistant varieties, space plants well, avoid excess nitrogen, and begin monitoring before fruiting.

Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.symptoms: Small purple spots enlarge and merge, giving leaves a scorched or reddish-brown appearance.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.causes: The fungus Diplocarpon earlianum survives on infected leaves and spreads in wet, humid conditions.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.treatment: Remove infected foliage after harvest, improve airflow, and use labeled fungicides if disease is severe.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.prevention: Use disease-free plants, avoid overhead irrigation, renovate beds properly, and remove old infected leaves.

Strawberry___healthy.symptoms: Leaves are green and vigorous without scorch, spots, mildew, or abnormal curling.
Strawberry___healthy.causes: Healthy plants are supported by clean transplants, good drainage, proper spacing, and balanced nutrition.
Strawberry___healthy.prevention: Use clean planting stock, mulch to reduce splash, avoid wet foliage, and remove old leaves after harvest.

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Small dark water-soaked leaf spots develop, often with yellow halos, and fruit may show raised scabby lesions.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.causes: Bacteria spread through infected seed, transplants, splashing water, tools, and warm wet weather.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.treatment: Remove infected tissue, avoid overhead irrigation, improve spacing, and use copper products only under local recommendations.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.prevention: Use certified seed and transplants, rotate crops, sanitize tools, mulch soil, and avoid handling wet plants.

Tomato___Early_blight.symptoms: Older leaves show brown target-like spots with yellowing; lower leaves may die and drop.
Tomato___Early_blight.causes: Alternaria fungi survive in soil and plant debris and spread during warm, humid conditions.
Tomato___Early_blight.treatment: Remove lower infected leaves, mulch soil, stake plants, and use labeled fungicides when needed.
Tomato___Early_blight.prevention: Rotate crops, avoid overhead watering, space plants well, remove debris, and keep foliage off the soil.

Tomato___Late_blight.symptoms: Leaves develop large water-soaked lesions that turn brown, with possible white growth during humid weather.
Tomato___Late_blight.causes: Phytophthora infestans spreads quickly in cool, wet weather from infected plants, volunteers, or windborne spores.
Tomato___Late_blight.treatment: Remove infected plants promptly and follow local late blight fungicide guidance during outbreaks.
Tomato___Late_blight.prevention: Use resistant varieties, improve airflow, avoid overhead watering, destroy volunteers, and track local disease alerts.

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.symptoms: Yellow patches appear on upper leaf surfaces with olive-gray mold on undersides, especially in humid environments.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.causes: Passalora fulva develops in high humidity, poor ventilation, and protected growing conditions such as greenhouses.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.treatment: Increase ventilation, remove infected leaves, reduce humidity, and apply labeled fungicides if needed.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.prevention: Space plants well, ventilate greenhouses, avoid wet foliage, use resistant varieties, and remove crop debris.

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.symptoms: Many small circular spots with gray centers and dark borders appear on lower leaves, causing yellowing and leaf drop.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.causes: Septoria fungi survive in debris and spread by splashing water during warm, wet weather.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.treatment: Remove infected lower leaves, mulch, stake plants, and apply labeled fungicides when disease is active.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.prevention: Rotate crops, remove tomato debris, avoid overhead watering, improve spacing, and control weeds in the nightshade family.

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.disease_name: Tomato - Spider mites Two-spotted spider mite
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.symptoms: Leaves show fine stippling, bronzing, webbing on undersides, curling, and possible leaf drop.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.causes: Two-spotted spider mites multiply rapidly in hot, dry conditions and on stressed plants.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.treatment: Spray leaf undersides with water, reduce plant stress, encourage beneficial insects, and use insecticidal soap or miticide when needed.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.prevention: Avoid drought stress, reduce dust, inspect leaf undersides, and avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that harm predators.

Tomato___Target_Spot.symptoms: Leaves develop brown spots with faint concentric rings, often surrounded by yellow tissue; fruit lesions may occur.
Tomato___Target_Spot.causes: Corynespora cassiicola survives in debris and spreads in warm, humid, wet conditions.
Tomato___Target_Spot.treatment: Remove infected leaves, improve airflow, avoid overhead irrigation, and apply labeled fungicides if disease progresses.
Tomato___Target_Spot.prevention: Rotate crops, remove debris, stake or prune plants, mulch soil, and use healthy transplants.

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.disease_name: Tomato - Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.symptoms: Leaves curl upward and yellow, plants become stunted, and fruit set may be poor.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.causes: The virus is spread mainly by whiteflies and through infected transplants.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.treatment: There is no cure; remove infected plants and manage whiteflies with integrated pest management.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.prevention: Use resistant varieties, control whiteflies, remove weeds and infected plants, and use insect netting where practical.

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.symptoms: Leaves show mosaic mottling, curling, distortion, reduced growth, and sometimes uneven fruit ripening.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.causes: The virus spreads through contaminated hands, tools, seed, plant debris, and infected transplants.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.treatment: There is no cure; remove infected plants and sanitize tools and hands before handling healthy plants.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.prevention: Use virus-free seed, disinfect tools, avoid tobacco contamination around tomatoes, and remove crop debris.

Tomato___healthy.symptoms: Leaves are green and normally shaped without spots, yellowing, curling, mildew, or mite damage.
Tomato___healthy.causes: Healthy tomato foliage comes from clean transplants, good airflow, proper watering, balanced fertility, and regular pest monitoring.
Tomato___healthy.treatment: No disease treatment is required; continue normal care and scouting.
Tomato___healthy.prevention: Rotate crops, mulch soil, stake plants, avoid overhead irrigation, prune for airflow, and inspect leaves weekly.
